skill.name: commit
skill.description: Guide an AI assistant through reviewing, staging, and creating conventional git commits with emoji prefixes and safety checks.
skill.summary: Create conventional git commits after reviewing changes and applying basic repository safety rules.
skill.author: Codename-11

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly true positives because the skill instructs git status, diff, log, add, and commit usage. The network and sensitive-file findings are false positives from documentation links and warnings not to commit secret files. No prompt injection, exfiltration logic, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Modifying Git Commands
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill instructs use of git add and git commit. This is expected for a commit assistant, but it can alter repository state if used without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly instruct git commands, including staging and committing. The context is legitimate, but the commands have real write effects.

low_findings.title: Documentation Link Misclassified As Network Activity
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The hardcoded Claude Code URL appears only in commit message attribution text. No active network request or data transfer is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are Markdown text inside a commit message template. I found no code that fetches the URL or sends data to it.

low_findings.title: Secret File Names Used In A Protective Rule
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The .env and credentials.json strings are listed as files that must not be committed. This reduces secret exposure risk instead of accessing secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a negative safety rule and does not instruct reading or uploading those files. No evidence of credential access was found.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Has No Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The cited lines discuss git commits, commit message format, and destructive command warnings. No weak cryptographic algorithm is named or used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA1, DES, or other weak crypto usage at the cited lines. The static result appears pattern-based.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples For Git Commit Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell command examples and command substitution for a multiline commit message. This pattern is not malicious here, but it should remain user-authorized.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell snippets are clearly present in Markdown and match the skill purpose. Risk comes from repository mutation, not from hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Manual commits can be inconsistent or miss important safety checks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through review, staging, and conventional commit messages.
actual_capabilities: Checks repository status before preparing a commit.
Reviews diffs to understand the change set.
Uses recent commit history as a style reference.
Stages relevant files when no files are staged.
Encourages splitting unrelated changes into separate commits.
limitations: Requires a git repository with command-line git available.
Does not push commits to remote repositories.
Does not bypass hooks unless the user explicitly requests it.
Cannot guarantee secret detection beyond the listed safety guidance.

use_cases.title: Commit completed feature work
use_cases.description: Prepare a clear conventional commit after a feature has been implemented and reviewed.

use_cases.title: Save documentation updates
use_cases.description: Create a focused documentation commit with an appropriate type and concise message.

use_cases.title: Separate mixed work
use_cases.description: Identify unrelated changes and turn them into smaller commits before sharing a branch.

prompt_templates.title: Commit my changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current git changes and create a clean conventional commit.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request after finishing a small task.

prompt_templates.title: Commit only staged files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use only the files that are already staged and create a conventional commit message.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has already selected the commit contents.

prompt_templates.title: Split unrelated changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the diff and tell me whether these changes should be split before committing.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs help keeping history focused.

prompt_templates.title: Match repository style
prompt_templates.prompt: Check recent commits, follow this repository's style, and create the best commit for the current changes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow that prioritizes local conventions.

output_examples.output: Creates a feature commit with an authentication-focused summary, optional body, Claude Code attribution, and co-author metadata.
Creates a documentation commit that summarizes the updated guides and avoids staging unrelated source files.

output_examples.input: Review these mixed changes before commit.
output: Reports whether the changes should be split.
Creates focused commits only after approval.

best_practices: Review the diff before allowing files to be staged.
Keep each commit focused on one logical change.
Check for secrets before committing generated or configuration files.
anti_patterns: Committing broad unrelated changes in one commit.
Skipping hooks without a clear user request.
Amending already pushed commits without confirming branch history.

faq.question: Does this skill push changes?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on local commits and does not instruct pushing to a remote repository.

faq.question: Can it create multiple commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends splitting work when changes cover different concerns, types, or file groups.

faq.question: Does it read recent commit history?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses recent commits as a style reference before creating a new message.

faq.question: Will it commit secret files?
faq.answer: It explicitly warns against committing .env and credentials.json files.

faq.question: Does it require user approval?
faq.answer: The skill says to ask first after completing a significant task before committing.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
